title: Tortilla española
subtitle: The Spanish potato omelette, golden outside and set but soft within.
excerpt: Potato and onion slow-cooked in olive oil, bound with beaten egg and cooked in a pan until set, then flipped and finished. The most satisfying use of four ingredients. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Tortilla española. The dish is documented in Spanish cookbooks from the early 19th century; the earliest written recipe is attributed to General Tomás de Zumalacárregui in 1817, recommending it as an economical dish. It remains one of the most widely eaten dishes in Spain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: A tortilla is excellent at room temperature and can be made up to 4 hours ahead.

content.text: The technique is the confiting of the potato in olive oil — not frying it at high heat, but cooking it slowly in barely-simmering oil until tender and yielding. The potato should not brown. After draining and combining with egg, the mixture is returned to the pan, set over low heat, and flipped once, carefully, to finish the second side.

The set is the debate. Many Spaniards prefer a tortilla slightly cuajada — cooked through but still moist at the centre. Others want it set firm. This recipe aims for just set, with a fraction of give at the middle.

items.prepNote: for confiting the potato; most is drained off

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a 24 to 26 cm frying pan over medium-low heat. Add {{potato}} sliced potato and {{onion}} sliced onion. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Cook gently for 20 to 25 minutes, turning occasionally with a spatula, until the potato is completely tender and can be cut with the edge of a spoon. The oil should bubble gently throughout; turn the heat down if it browns. Drain off almost all the oil into a heatproof bowl, reserving 2 tablespoons.
Combine and cook the first side

Beat {{eggs}} eggs in a large bowl. Add the potato and onion mixture and press gently to combine. The potato should break up slightly but not mash completely. Add the reserved oil back to the pan over medium heat. Pour in the egg and potato mixture. Tilt the pan to spread it evenly. Cook for 5 to 6 minutes until the edges are set and the centre is still slightly soft on top.

Place a large flat plate over the pan. In one confident movement, flip the pan so the tortilla drops onto the plate. Slide it back into the pan, cooked side up. Cook for 3 to 4 more minutes until the second side is set. Slide onto a plate and leave to rest for 5 minutes. Serve at room temperature or warm, cut into wedges.

items.symptom: Potato not cooked through after 25 minutes
items.cause: Oil not hot enough, or slices too thick.
items.fix: The oil must be hot enough to bubble around the potato — low simmering, not cold poaching. Slice potato to 3 mm; thicker slices take much longer.

items.symptom: Tortilla sticking and tearing on the flip
items.cause: Pan not properly seasoned, or base not set before flipping.
items.fix: The tortilla must be fully set at the base and around the edges before flipping. Run a spatula around the sides to check. A well-seasoned cast-iron pan is the most reliable for the flip.

items.cause: Overcooked, or too many eggs relative to potato.
items.fix: Remove the tortilla when the centre still has a slight softness. The residual heat will set it further as it rests.

content.text: The tortilla española is in every bar in Spain. Cut into cubes on toothpicks, served in a bocadillo, eaten at room temperature at a tapas table at midnight: it appears in all contexts and at all hours. The debate about whether to add onion (con cebolla or sin cebolla) is as unresolved as any in Spanish gastronomy. This recipe includes onion, as most Spanish home cooks do.
